verdict: Substantial prior art HITS in metallurgy/materials-science domain (Query 2): nine results explicitly tie neural networks AND Bauschinger effect / cyclic plasticity / kinematic hardening. The candidate's metallurgy-side mapping (Bauschinger / kinematic hardening of stress-strain in metals) is textbook and already modeled by NNs. The LLM/RLHF-side queries (1, 3, 4) yield zero keyword hits and no functionally equivalent prior art formalizing RLHF as cyclic plasticity. Novelty therefore plausibly survives at the cross-domain mapping level, but the metallurgy half is well-known and several of the matched papers (especially 1007/BF01212634 and the PNAS/Nature deep-learning-predicts-plasticity papers) already use neural networks to model the exact phenomenon being borrowed; the cross-domain transfer to RLHF is the only remaining novel piece.

disagreements.note: Prior agent calls RLHF dual-objective conflict substantive prior art. I am skeptical: this is generic RLHF conflict, not Bauschinger / kinematic hardening / back-stress formalism. Zero keyword overlap; no functional equivalence to the proposed plasticity-theoretic mapping.

Prior agent treats 'Asymmetric Confidence Penalties' as strong prior art on asymmetric over-correction. Skeptical view: ACE is asymmetric penalty over confidence, not asymmetric softening from prior loading direction — the Bauschinger phenomenon is structurally different (history-dependent yield-surface translation). Author's own novelty_claim already lists this paper as a related-but-distinct contribution.

Prior agent calls loss-of-plasticity adjacent prior art. Skeptical view: 'loss of plasticity' here means networks losing ability to learn anything — orthogonal to direction-dependent asymmetric softening. 'plasticity' alone does not satisfy 'plastic deformation' substring rule.

Push-Pull OT alignment is a mitigation technique, not a Bauschinger-style mechanistic model. Skeptical: terminological similarity ('push-pull') is not functional equivalence to back-stress / kinematic hardening.

Equilibrate RLHF describes overly-safe trade-off qualitatively; no kinematic-hardening / back-stress formalism. Author cites this as documenting empirical phenomenon, distinct from proposed contribution.

SafeGrad is gradient-surgery mitigation; conflicting gradients are NOT the same as accumulated back-stress in plastic deformation analogy. Author already explicitly lists this as related-but-distinct.
Conflict-aware reward modeling; addresses proxy-policy conflict at reward level, not direction-dependent asymmetric softening with state variables.

Emergent misalignment from narrow fine-tuning documents an asymmetric phenomenon but with NO formal cyclic-plasticity / kinematic-hardening apparatus. Author cites this paper as documenting empirical asymmetric softening — novelty claim is the FORMALISM not the phenomenon.
Aligned/harmful direction analysis is empirical; no Bauschinger / back-stress / yield-surface translation. Author already cites in novelty_claim.
Foundational paper on safety compromise via fine-tuning — empirical, no plasticity-theoretic model. Author already cites in novelty_claim.

comparison_with_07_hit_miss.agreements_summary: Both verdicts agree on all 9 metallurgy-domain HITs from Query 2 (forced by rule) and on the no-hit verdict for Safe RLHF repos / overview / multimodal / sociotechnical / Show-Don't-Tell papers. Disagreement is entirely in LLM-side judgment-hits: prior agent generously counts any RLHF directional-conflict / asymmetric-softening paper as substantive prior art; I apply the skeptical 'functionally equivalent prior art' standard and require the specific Bauschinger / kinematic-hardening / back-stress formalism, which none of the LLM-side papers provide.
